main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (carrot, peas, beans, cauliflower, potato, corn)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Subzi Navratan is approximately 250 g, served as a main dish alongside rice or Indian bread.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Mixed Vegetable Curry / Navratan Korma composite
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry for Navratan Subzi exists. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry and vegetable korma entries, weighted for the typical ingredient composition of Navratan Subzi (nine mixed vegetables, paneer, cream, cashew, raisins, mild spiced sauce). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used to cross-check individual ingredient contributions for cream, cashew, and paneer fractions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed vegetable curry analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Navratan Subzi found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed vegetable curry entries in the Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) International Tables of GI and GL. The dish is dominated by non-starchy vegetables, paneer, and cream, which collectively suppress glycemic response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (45 × 8.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 9.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~280 mg/100 g; ~700 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 250 g restaurant serving of Subzi Navratan contributes approximately 700 mg sodium, representing about 30% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and moderate carbohydrate content (~8.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Subzi Navratan has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of about 9.6 per serving, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with low-GI accompaniments such as roti or basmati rice in controlled portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cream and paneer (~2.8 g/100 g; ~7 g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The cream and paneer content of Subzi Navratan contributes approximately 7 g of saturated fat per serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption or request preparation with reduced cream and substitution of low-fat dairy to align with saturated fat targets below 7% of total energy intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Subzi Navratan, also known as Navratan Subzi or Navratan Korma, is a classic North Indian vegetarian curry whose name translates to 'nine jewels vegetables', referring to the nine or more assorted vegetables, fruits, and nuts traditionally used in its preparation. The dish typically includes a colourful combination of carrots, peas, green beans, cauliflower, potato, corn, and sometimes capsicum, enriched with paneer (Indian cottage cheese), cashew nuts, and raisins, all simmered in a mild, creamy tomato-onion sauce flavoured with cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala. The sauce is finished with cream or yoghurt, giving it a rich, velvety texture and a subtly sweet, mildly spiced flavour profile. Subzi Navratan is a popular festive and restaurant dish across India and is widely served in Indian restaurants in Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~98 kcal/100 g), meaningful dietary fibre (~2.1 g/100 g) from its vegetable base, and calcium and protein from paneer. Its low glycemic index (~45) makes it relatively suitable for blood glucose management. The sodium content (~280 mg/100 g) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension, and the saturated fat from cream and paneer (~2.8 g/100 g) is relevant for cardiovascular health. It is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian, and is typically served with naan, roti, or basmati rice.

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN or HPB entry for Navratan Subzi exists. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry and vegetable korma entries with USDA cross-check for cream, cashew, and paneer fractions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using comparable mixed vegetable curry entries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-documented and the source adaptation is methodologically sound.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated or reported numeric values where defensible source data was available. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement for Navratan Subzi has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. Sodium value reflects typical restaurant preparation; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. The dish is served at Indian House / Dumpukht Grills & Curry, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey data source record GD003422). Multilingual names follow Singaporean cuisine rules: English (food_name), Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are all provided. No zero-value core macros are present in this record.
